advantages: Average O(1) insert, lookup, delete by key — the gold standard for label-driven access.
Preserves insertion order (since 3.7), so iteration is predictable.
Built into the language; no extra dependency needed.
disadvantages: Higher memory overhead than a list of the same length.
Keys must be hashable — mutable values (list/dict/set) can't be keys.
Order-by-key requires sorting; dicts aren't sorted maps.
Worst-case O(n) operations exist if hash collisions are pathological (very rare in practice).
